scope: Hong Kong DSE Chemistry curriculum (S4-S6)
Compulsory part (12 topics) and elective part (choose 2 of 3 topics)
Public exam: Paper 1 (compulsory), Paper 2 (elective), SBA (practical/inquiry skills)
out_of_scope: Non-DSE chemistry curricula unless explicitly compared
University-level chemistry
Teacher pedagogy, school administration, resource selection
classification_hints: Use this root only when no more specific topic fits
Prefer compulsory/elective subdivision nodes for broad queries

scope: Twelve compulsory topics assessed in Paper 1
Approximately 182 curriculum hours
out_of_scope: Elective topics (Paper 2 only)
Topic 16 investigative study (SBA context only)
classification_hints: Route ordinary chemistry content here unless it clearly matches an elective
Use the specific topic node, not this parent, for final classification

scope: Atmosphere, oceans and crust as sources of chemicals
Physical separation methods: evaporation, distillation, crystallisation, filtration
Chemical tests for water and sodium/chloride in salt
Chemical changes: thermal decomposition of silver oxide, action of heat/water/acid on calcium carbonate
Conservation of natural resources and safe disposal
out_of_scope: Detailed atmospheric chemistry beyond composition
Industrial extraction methods for metals (covered in Metals and Redox)
Quantitative separation yield calculations
classification_hints: Look for keywords: sea water, salt extraction, distillation, crystallisation, filtration, natural resources
Items about separating sand/salt/sugar mixtures belong here
common_misconceptions: Confusing filtration with evaporation
Thinking all mixtures can be separated by a single method

common_misclassifications.reason: Metal ores may appear, but extraction methods and reactivity are in Metals.

overlap_links.notes: Natural resources link to metal ores and recycling.

scope: Atomic structure: protons, neutrons, electrons; atomic number and mass number
Electronic structure and the periodic table
Ionic, covalent and metallic bonding; electron diagrams
Structure-property relationships for giant ionic, giant covalent and simple molecular substances
Chemical formulae and naming of ionic/covalent compounds
Relative atomic/molecular mass and basic stoichiometric ideas
out_of_scope: Polarity and intermolecular forces (Microscopic World II)
Mole calculations beyond formula mass (Metals / Acids)
Shapes of molecules / VSEPR (Microscopic World II)
classification_hints: Look for: atomic structure, bonding types, electron diagrams, formula/naming, structure-property links
Distinguish from Microscopic World II by absence of polarity, IMF, hydrogen bonding
common_misconceptions: Intermolecular forces are chemical bonds
Ionic compounds form molecules

common_misclassifications.reason: Both cover bonding and structure; II adds polarity, IMF, hydrogen bonding and VSEPR.

overlap_links.notes: Extends bonding to polarity and intermolecular forces.
Bonding and formulae needed for metal reactions and stoichiometry.

scope: Occurrence of metals and metal ores; metal recycling
Reactions of common metals with oxygen, water and dilute acids
Metal reactivity series and displacement reactions
Mole concept, Avogadro constant, molar mass, empirical and molecular formulae
Stoichiometric calculations including reacting masses and limiting reagents
Metal corrosion and rust prevention
out_of_scope: Detailed metallurgy/electrolytic extraction (Redox/Electrolysis)
Transition metal chemistry (Patterns in the Chemical World)
Alloys (Materials Chemistry elective)
classification_hints: Look for: reactivity series, displacement, mole calculations, empirical formula, rusting, recycling
Items requiring mole stoichiometry for the first time often belong here
common_misconceptions: All metals react with water
Rust is the same as general corrosion

common_misclassifications.reason: Displacement reactions are redox, but systematic redox/electrolysis is in topic 7.

overlap_links.notes: Redox explanations of metal displacement and corrosion.

scope: Acid and base properties; hydroxide ions and hydrogen ions
Indicators (litmus, methyl orange, phenolphthalein) and pH scale
Strong/weak acids and alkalis; neutralisation
Molar concentration and related calculations
Acid-base titrations and standard solutions
Preparation of salts by neutralisation, carbonate reaction, direct combination
out_of_scope: Buffer solutions and pH calculations involving Ka (beyond guide)
Organic acid reactions beyond simple neutralisation (Carbon Compounds)
classification_hints: Look for: pH, indicator colour changes, titration, salt preparation, neutralisation, molarity
Distinguish from Redox by absence of oxidation number/electron transfer
common_misconceptions: Strong acids have the highest pH
Neutralisation always gives pH 7 for weak acid/alkali salts

common_misclassifications.reason: Neutralisation enthalpy appears in energetics, but acid-base concepts belong here.

overlap_links.notes: Enthalpy of neutralisation links the two topics.

scope: Coal, petroleum and natural gas as fossil fuels and hydrocarbon sources
Fractional distillation of petroleum; properties of fractions
Combustion of hydrocarbons and environmental issues (air pollution, acid rain, global warming)
Alkanes and alkenes: structure, reactions (combustion, substitution, addition), cracking
Alcohols and carboxylic acids: structure, naming and simple reactions
out_of_scope: Full organic reaction mechanisms (Carbon Compounds topic)
Detailed polymer chemistry (Materials Chemistry elective / Carbon Compounds)
Industrial synthesis of methanol (Industrial Chemistry elective)
classification_hints: Look for: fossil fuels, fractional distillation, alkanes, alkenes, cracking, alcohols, carboxylic acids, environmental effects
Basic organic chemistry before the systematic Carbon Compounds topic
common_misconceptions: Cracking only produces alkenes
All hydrocarbons are alkanes

common_misclassifications.reason: Both cover carbon compounds; Carbon Compounds is the deeper systematic treatment including isomerism and more functional groups.

overlap_links.notes: Systematic organic chemistry builds on this introductory topic.

scope: Electronegativity and polar covalent bonds
Molecular polarity and shapes of simple molecules
Intermolecular forces: van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding
Structure and properties of ice
Giant covalent structures: diamond, graphite, silicon dioxide
out_of_scope: Detailed VSEPR beyond simple molecules
Quantum mechanical explanations of bonding
classification_hints: Look for: electronegativity, polar bonds, molecular polarity, hydrogen bonding, IMF, ice structure, diamond/graphite
Distinguish from Microscopic World I by presence of polarity/IMF
common_misconceptions: Hydrogen bonding occurs in any molecule containing hydrogen
van der Waals forces are stronger than covalent bonds

common_misclassifications.reason: Both are structure/bonding topics; II covers polarity and intermolecular forces.

overlap_links.notes: Bond breaking/forming links to enthalpy changes.

topics.topic_name: Redox Reactions, Chemical Cells and Electrolysis
scope: Simple chemical cells: metal electrodes, electrolytes, salt bridge, electrode potentials/voltages
Oxidation and reduction in terms of oxygen/hydrogen/electron transfer and oxidation numbers
Common oxidising/reducing agents and their observations
Electrolysis: cells, preferential discharge, anode/cathode reactions, electroplating
Industrial electrolysis context (aluminium extraction, chlor-alkali mentioned in elective)
out_of_scope: Standard electrode potentials and Nernst equation
Detailed industrial chlor-alkali cells (Industrial Chemistry elective)
Fuel cell mechanisms beyond simple recognition
classification_hints: Look for: oxidation number, redox equations, chemical cells, electrolysis, electroplating, displacement
If item involves both redox and electrochemical cells, route here rather than Metals
common_misconceptions: Oxidation always involves oxygen
In electrolysis the anode is always positive

common_misclassifications.reason: Metal displacement is redox, but systematic electrochemistry belongs here.

overlap_links.notes: Electrolysis underpins chlor-alkali industry in elective.

scope: Energy changes in chemical reactions; exothermic and endothermic reactions
Enthalpy change as heat change at constant pressure
Standard enthalpy changes of reaction, combustion, formation and neutralisation
Simple calorimetry experiments
Hess's law and enthalpy cycle calculations
out_of_scope: Bomb calorimetry
Born-Haber cycles
Entropy and Gibbs free energy
classification_hints: Look for: enthalpy, exothermic/endothermic, calorimetry, Hess's law, standard enthalpy changes
If item is about heat change rather than reaction mechanism, route here
common_misconceptions: Breaking bonds releases energy
Exothermic reactions have positive enthalpy change

common_misclassifications.reason: Both discuss energy; kinetics focuses on activation energy and rate, energetics on overall enthalpy change.

overlap_links.notes: Activation energy is in kinetics; overall enthalpy is here.

scope: Methods to follow reaction progress (titration, gas volume/pressure, mass, colour, turbidity)
Average and instantaneous rates; initial rate
Factors affecting rate: concentration, temperature, surface area, catalysts
Molar volume of gas at room temperature and pressure (24 dm³)
out_of_scope: Rate equations and orders (Industrial Chemistry elective)
Arrhenius equation (Industrial Chemistry elective)
Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution (Industrial Chemistry elective)
classification_hints: Look for: rate of reaction, factors affecting rate, initial rate, gas volume measurements
If item asks about rate equation or order, route to Industrial Chemistry elective
common_misconceptions: Catalysts change the enthalpy change of a reaction
Increasing concentration always changes rate constant

common_misclassifications.reason: Industrial Chemistry covers rate equations, activation energy and Maxwell-Boltzmann in depth.

overlap_links.notes: Rate equations and catalysis are extended in Industrial Chemistry.

scope: Reversible and irreversible reactions; dynamic equilibrium
Equilibrium constant Kc in terms of concentration
Effect of temperature and concentration on equilibrium position
Relationship between enthalpy sign and temperature effect on Kc
out_of_scope: Effect of adding inert species on equilibrium
Redox and acid-base equilibrium systems in depth
Kp and partial pressures
classification_hints: Look for: equilibrium constant Kc, Le Chatelier, dynamic equilibrium, reversible reactions
If item involves Kp or inert gas effects, it is out of scope
common_misconceptions: At equilibrium the concentrations of reactants and products are equal
A catalyst shifts the equilibrium position

common_misclassifications.reason: Industrial processes apply equilibrium, but the industrial context belongs to the elective.

overlap_links.notes: Haber process links equilibrium and industrial chemistry.

scope: Homologous series, structural/formula/naming for alkanes, alkenes, haloalkanes, alcohols, aldehydes/ketones, carboxylic acids, esters, amides, primary amines
Structural, cis-trans and optical isomerism (basic recognition)
Reactions of the listed functional groups
Addition and condensation polymers (nylon, polyester)
out_of_scope: Full reaction mechanisms beyond curly-arrow descriptions where specified
Aromatic chemistry beyond recognition of benzene as feedstock
Amino acid/protein chemistry (Materials Chemistry elective context)
classification_hints: Look for: functional groups, organic reactions, isomerism, polymers, nomenclature
Distinguish from Fossil Fuels by level of depth and range of functional groups
common_misconceptions: Alkenes undergo substitution with bromine
Esters are produced by addition polymerisation

common_misclassifications.reason: Fossil Fuels introduces alkanes/alkenes/alcohols/acids; Carbon Compounds is the systematic treatment.

overlap_links.notes: Materials Chemistry extends polymer and material science aspects.
Analytical Chemistry includes tests for organic functional groups.

scope: Periodic trends in physical properties (bonding, melting point, conductivity) across Period 3 (Na–Ar)
Trends in bonding, stoichiometry and acid-base character of oxides Na2O to Cl2O
Transition metals: coloured ions, variable oxidation states, catalytic properties
out_of_scope: Detailed thermodynamic explanation of periodic trends
Complex ion chemistry of transition metals
classification_hints: Look for: period 3 trends, oxide acid-base character, transition metal colours/oxidation states
Do not route simple metal reactivity here; that is Metals
common_misconceptions: All period 3 oxides are acidic
Transition metals are only those in the d-block

common_misclassifications.reason: Metals covers reactivity and extraction; this topic covers periodic trends and transition metals.

scope: Three elective topics; students choose two
Assessed in Paper 2 (20% of public exam)
out_of_scope: Compulsory part content
A fourth elective option (the curriculum has three electives)
classification_hints: Use this parent only for broad queries about electives
Route to the specific elective topic for final classification

scope: Rate equations, reaction order and rate constant
Activation energy and Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution
Catalysis and industrial processes
Haber process, chlor-alkali industry, methanol synthesis
Green chemistry principles and atom economy
out_of_scope: General compulsory kinetics (topic 9)
Fuel cells and batteries (compulsory Redox only)
classification_hints: Look for: rate equation, order, activation energy, Arrhenius, Haber process, chlor-alkali, atom economy
If item is about industrial conditions and equilibrium yield, route here
common_misconceptions: Higher temperature always increases yield
Catalysts change equilibrium position

common_misclassifications.reason: Compulsory kinetics covers factors qualitatively; Industrial Chemistry adds rate equations and activation energy quantitatively.
Industrial processes apply equilibrium, but rate/economic factors make this Industrial Chemistry.

overlap_links.notes: Haber process links to equilibrium principles.

scope: Natural polymers: cellulose, chitin
Synthetic polymers: addition/condensation polymerisation, thermoplastics/thermosets, biodegradable plastics
Metals and alloys: crystal packing, unit cells, coordination number, alloy properties
Liquid crystals and nanomaterials
Green chemistry in materials production
out_of_scope: Simple polymer recognition in Carbon Compounds (compulsory)
General metal reactivity (Metals topic)
classification_hints: Look for: polymer structure/processing, alloys, liquid crystals, nanomaterials, biodegradable plastics
Distinguish from Carbon Compounds by emphasis on materials and properties rather than reaction pathways
common_misconceptions: All plastics are thermoplastics
Nanomaterials are only carbon-based

common_misclassifications.reason: Carbon Compounds covers functional group reactions; Materials Chemistry covers polymer/alloy/materials science.

overlap_links.notes: Polymer concepts build on addition/condensation reactions.

scope: Chemical tests for cations, anions, gases and organic functional groups
Separation and purification: crystallisation, distillation/fractional distillation, solvent extraction, chromatography (Rf, melting/boiling point purity)
Quantitative analysis: volumetric analysis including acid-base and redox titrations
Instrumental methods: colorimetry, IR spectroscopy (functional group identification), mass spectrometry (molecular structure and simple fragmentation)
out_of_scope: Detailed instrument operation principles
NMR spectroscopy
General separation methods in Earth topic (physical only)
classification_hints: Look for: qualitative analysis, chromatography, titration, IR spectrum, mass spectrum, colorimetry
If item is about identifying an unknown compound using tests/spectra, route here
common_misconceptions: IR spectroscopy determines molecular mass
Chromatography Rf values are always the same regardless of solvent

common_misclassifications.reason: Acid-base titrations are a subset; Analytical Chemistry includes redox titrations and instrumental methods too.
Earth covers simple physical separation; Analytical Chemistry covers systematic qualitative/quantitative/instrumental analysis.

overlap_links.notes: Organic functional group tests require prior knowledge of functional groups.

scope: Inquiry-based project work supporting SBA
Experimental design, data collection, error analysis, reporting
out_of_scope: Not a public-exam content topic
Not a standalone topic for item classification unless explicitly about practical investigation design
classification_hints: Only use when the prompt is about designing/carrying out a chemistry investigation
If the item is a written exam-style question, route to the relevant compulsory/elective topic
common_misconceptions: Investigative Study is examined as a topic in Paper 1 or Paper 2

common_misclassifications.reason: Both involve practical skills; Analytical Chemistry is an assessed elective content topic.
